figures.caption: Figure 6. Sensitivity of the IQEMC spectral gap to presampling. For an n = 6 system at T = 0.1 and C = 0.15, the energy Emax and E0 are estimated from various uniformly sampled states. The spectral gap remains nearly unchanged, demonstrating the robustness to coarse energy-range estimates.

Figure 8. Spectral gap of IQEMC and a state-blind randomk control in fully connected random Ising models (T = 0.1, C = 0.15). The random-k control (red) uses sweep rates sampled from the distribution in IQEMC (green) but removes the energy-dependence. As the system size increases, the advantage of IQEMC becomes more pronounced, implying that energy-dependent irreversibility is the primary source of its acceleration.

Figure 2. Energy-adaptive proposals via Landau–Zener transitions enhance sampling efficiency. (a) Landau–Zener modulation of the proposal distribution with various sweep rates k. (b) For high-energy and low-energy initial states in the 1D Ising chain (n = 6, T = 0.1, C = 0.15), IQEMC employs an adaptive proposal distribution (green), whereas QEMC uses a fixed proposal distribution (red). (c) The proposal distribution for high-energy states in IQEMC (green) enables large-∆E transitions. (d) For low-energy states, IQEMC favors small-∆E transitions. QEMC (red) yields local moves across all energies.

Figure 4. Convergence benchmark for fully connected random Ising models at T = 0.1 and C = 0.15. (a) Spectral gap versus spin number n. IQEMC (green) outperforms the three baselines (gray: uniform, orange: annealing, red: QEMC) across 150 instances. (b) Spectral gap versus temperature for n = 6. IQEMC exhibits advantages at low temperatures. (c) Fraction of spectral-gap improvement by IQEMC over the three baselines. As the system size n increases, IQEMC’s acceleration becomes more dominant.

Figure 5. Convergence of the average magnetization over 10 independent realizations for a single instance at T = 0.1. (a) Magnetization versus MCMC steps for uniform sampling (gray), QEMC (red), and IQEMC (green) on a fully connected random Ising model (n = 6). (b) Results for the three methods combined with the strategy of parallel tempering. IQEMC has the fastest convergence in both cases.
